cluster_name: Algeria orphanage fire kills 11 including children amid heatwave
event_summary: A fire broke out before dawn on 16 July 2026 at a state-run facility in Mohammadia, near Algiers, killing 11 people including children and injuring 19 others. Prime Minister Sifi Ghrieb visited survivors at hospitals; President Tebboune offered condolences from Berlin.
tldr: Coverage was broadly factual across 10 countries and 13 outlets with minimal editorial divergence — the story's status as a clear-cut tragedy kept frames tightly anchored to casualties and the official response. RT Africa stood apart as the only outlet to contextualise the fire within Algeria's prior institutional safety record, citing a 2019 maternity ward blaze that killed eight babies, casting this as a pattern rather than an isolated incident. Multiple outlets embedded the fire within Algeria's acute wildfire emergency — nearly 1,000 fires recorded in one week — raising an implicit question about whether overwhelmed emergency services affected response capacity, though no outlet made that link explicit. BBC Africa was the only outlet to report the opposition Jil Jadid party's call for a transparent investigation and legal accountability for negligence, and also the sole source of an eyewitness account ('We heard the fire engines arriving and the children screaming'). Sky News UK's headline 'Eleven children killed' overshot the official position, which stated only that children were 'among' the 11 dead — a potential accuracy discrepancy not found in any other outlet.

evidence.quote: En Algérie, onze corps dont certains calcinés, ont été retrouvés dans un orphelinat près de la banlieue d'Alger. Le bâtiment a été ravagé par les flammes. Plusieurs enfants sont parmi les victimes.
At least 11 people, including children, have been killed and 19 others injured in a fire at an orphanage in the Algerian capital, the North African nation’s authorities said on Thursday.
Eleven people have died in a fire at a foster care facility in Algiers, Algeria's capital city.

A fire burning at an orphanage in Algeria's capital has killed at least 11 people including children, authorities said Thursday, amid a heatwave that has sparked hundreds of blazes in the country's north.
Algeria has been experiencing a heatwave for several days, and nearly 1,000 fires have been recorded in the space of a week, AFP said.
On Thursday, the Civil Protection service said its crews had responded to 115 forest, scrubland, agricultural, and palm-grove fires over the previous 24 hours.
Algeria has been sweltering under a heatwave for several days, and nearly 1,000 fires have been recorded in the space of a week

frames.sub_frame: government condolences and PM hospital visits

evidence.quote: Algerian Prime Minister Sifi Ghrieb speaks with survivors after a fire burning at an orphanage in the country's capital has killed at least 11 people, including children, according to authorities.
President Abdelmadjid Tebboune expressed his condolences amid his trip to Berlin.
He also ordered the authorities to provide comprehensive medical and psychological care for the injured and support for the bereaved families.
the opposition Jil Jadid party has called for a transparent investigation into fire and legal accountability for any negligence or safety failures.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to reference the 2019 El Oued hospital fire that killed eight babies, framing the orphanage blaze as part of a pattern of Algerian institutional fire safety failures rather than an isolated incident.

Only outlet to include an eyewitness account: nearby resident Abdessalam Merrah told AFP, 'At around 03:00, we heard the fire engines arriving and the children screaming. We helped as much as we could, but unfortunately we were told that 11 people had already died.'

Only outlet to name and quote the opposition Jil Jadid party's demand for 'a transparent investigation into fire and legal accountability for any negligence or safety failures', adding a political-accountability dimension absent from all other corpus items.

Headline reads 'Eleven children killed in fire at Algerian orphanage', stating all 11 dead were children. Every other outlet reported that children were 'among' the dead — a materially different and potentially inaccurate claim.

Headline 'Orphanage fire kills 11 as Algeria struggles in heatwave' is the only headline to make an explicit causal link between the fire and the heatwave crisis, contextualising the deaths within a systemic environmental emergency.
Provided the day's granular wildfire operations breakdown: 89 fires fully extinguished, 14 under guard after being suppressed, 14 remaining active — detail absent from all other outlets.

bottom_line: Thirteen international outlets across 10 countries converged on the same factual casualty-and-response frame with near-zero editorial divergence, treating the orphanage fire as an unambiguous tragedy rather than a contested event. The most analytically significant variation was RT Africa's historical framing — linking this blaze to Algeria's 2019 institutional fire record — and the broadly shared but rarely explicit subtext that Algeria's emergency services were simultaneously managing a near-record wildfire emergency during the same heatwave, a structural context no outlet directly interrogated.
